The [Judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Sweden "Judiciary of Sweden") is independent from the Riksdag, Government and other State administrative authorities.[[165]](#cite_note-The_Swedish_courts-175) The role of [judicial review](/wiki/Judicial_review "Judicial review") of legislation is not practised by the courts; instead, the [Council on Legislation](/wiki/Council_on_Legislation_(Sweden) "Council on Legislation (Sweden)") gives non-binding opinions on legality.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrill2009243-176) There is no [stare decisis](/wiki/Stare_decisis "Stare decisis") in that courts are not bound by [precedent](/wiki/Precedent "Precedent"), although it is influential.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrill2009246-177)

### Elections

Main articles: [List of political parties in Sweden](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Sweden "List of political parties in Sweden") and [Elections in Sweden](/wiki/Elections_in_Sweden "Elections in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Riksdag.ipred_b9dn510_4451.jpg/250px-Riksdag.ipred_b9dn510_4451.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riksdag.ipred_b9dn510_4451.jpg)

The [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") chamber, at the time of a vote, in 2009

For over 50 years, Sweden had had five parties who continually received enough votes to gain seats in the Riksdag—the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Swedish_Social_Democratic_Party "Swedish Social Democratic Party"), the [Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party"), the [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Sweden) "Centre Party (Sweden)"), the [Liberal People's Party](/wiki/Liberal_People%27s_Party_(Sweden) "Liberal People's Party (Sweden)") and the [Left Party](/wiki/Left_Party_(Sweden) "Left Party (Sweden)")—before the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_(Sweden) "Green Party (Sweden)") became the sixth party in the [1988 election](/wiki/1988_Swedish_general_election "1988 Swedish general election"). In the 1991 election, while the Greens lost their seats, two new parties gained seats for the first time: the [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_Democrats_(Sweden) "Christian Democrats (Sweden)") and [New Democracy](/wiki/New_Democracy_(Sweden) "New Democracy (Sweden)"). It was not until elections in 2010 that an eighth party, the [Sweden Democrats](/wiki/Sweden_Democrats "Sweden Democrats"), gained Riksdag seats. In the elections to the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"), parties who have failed to pass the Riksdag threshold have managed to gain representation at that venue: the [June List](/wiki/June_List "June List") ([2004–2009](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_Sweden "2004 European Parliament election in Sweden")), the [Pirate Party](/wiki/Pirate_Party_(Sweden) "Pirate Party (Sweden)") ([2009–2014](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_Sweden "2009 European Parliament election in Sweden")), and [Feminist Initiative](/wiki/Feminist_Initiative_(Sweden) "Feminist Initiative (Sweden)") ([2014–2019](/wiki/2014_European_Parliament_election_in_Sweden "2014 European Parliament election in Sweden")).

Election turnout in Sweden has always been high by international comparison. Although it declined in recent decades, the latest elections saw an increase in voter turnout (80.11% in [2002](/wiki/2002_Sweden_general_election "2002 Sweden general election"), 81.99% in 2006, 84.63% in 2010, 85.81 in [2014](/wiki/2014_Swedish_general_election "2014 Swedish general election"))[[168]](#cite_note-178) and 87.18% in [2018](/wiki/2018_Swedish_general_election "2018 Swedish general election").[[169]](#cite_note-179) Swedish politicians enjoyed a high degree of confidence from the citizens in the 1960s, However, that level of confidence has since declined steadily, and is now at a markedly lower level than in its Scandinavian neighbours.[[170]](#cite_note-180)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Regions of Sweden](/wiki/Regions_of_Sweden "Regions of Sweden"), [Municipalities of Sweden](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Sweden "Municipalities of Sweden"), [County Administrative Boards of Sweden](/wiki/County_Administrative_Boards_of_Sweden "County Administrative Boards of Sweden"), and [Administrative divisions of Sweden](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Sweden "Administrative divisions of Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/SWE-Map_Kommuner2007.svg/250px-SWE-Map_Kommuner2007.svg.png)](/wiki/File:SWE-Map_Kommuner2007.svg)

Municipal divisions of Sweden

Sweden is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") divided into 21 regions (*regioner*) and 290 municipalities (*kommuner*). Every region corresponds to a [county](/wiki/Counties_of_Sweden "Counties of Sweden") (*län*) with a number of municipalities per county. Regions and municipalities are both local governments but have different roles and separate responsibilities. Health care, public transport and certain cultural institutions are administered by regional councils. Preschools, primary and secondary schooling, public water utilities, garbage disposal, elderly care and rescue services are administered by the municipalities. [Gotland](/wiki/Gotland "Gotland") is a special case of being a region with only one municipality and the functions of region and municipality are performed by the same organisation.[[171]](#cite_note-181)

Municipal and region government in Sweden is similar to [city commission](/wiki/City_commission_government "City commission government") and [cabinet-style council](/wiki/Cabinet-style_council "Cabinet-style council") government. Both levels have legislative assemblies ([municipal councils](/wiki/Municipal_council_(Sweden) "Municipal council (Sweden)") and region assemblies of between 31 and 101 members (always an uneven number) that are elected from [party-list proportional representation](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation") at the general election which are held every four years in conjunction with the national parliamentary elections.

Municipalities are also divided into a total of 2,512 [parishes](/wiki/Parishes_of_Sweden "Parishes of Sweden") (*församlingar*). These have no official political responsibilities but are traditional subdivisions of the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden") and still have some importance as census districts.

The Swedish central government has 21 [County Administrative Boards](/wiki/County_Administrative_Boards_of_Sweden "County Administrative Boards of Sweden") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *länsstyrelser*), which are responsible for regional state administration not assigned to other government agencies or local government. Each county administrative board is led by a [County Governor](/wiki/County_governors_of_Sweden "County governors of Sweden") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *landshövding*) appointed for a term of six years. The list of previous officeholders for the counties stretches back, in most cases, to 1634 when the counties were created by [Lord High Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_High_Chancellor_of_Sweden "Lord High Chancellor of Sweden") Count [Axel Oxenstierna](/wiki/Axel_Oxenstierna "Axel Oxenstierna"). The main responsibility of the County Administrative Board is to co-ordinate the development of the county in line with goals set by the Riksdag and Government.

There are older historical divisions, primarily the twenty-five [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Sweden "Provinces of Sweden") and three [lands](/wiki/Lands_of_Sweden "Lands of Sweden"), which still retain cultural significance.

### Judicial system

Main article: [Judiciary of Sweden](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Sweden "Judiciary of Sweden")

The courts are divided into two parallel and separate systems: The general courts (*allmänna domstolar*) for criminal and civil cases, and general administrative courts (*allmänna förvaltningsdomstolar*) for cases relating to disputes between private persons and the authorities.[[172]](#cite_note-182) Each of these systems has three tiers, where the top tier court of the respective system typically only will hear cases that may become [precedent](/wiki/Precedent "Precedent"). There are also several special courts, each of which hears a narrower range of cases as defined by legislation. Although these courts rule independently, some operate as specialised divisions within the general or general administrative courts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Hogsta_domstolen_Stockholm.jpg/250px-Hogsta_domstolen_Stockholm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hogsta_domstolen_Stockholm.jpg)

[Bonde Palace](/wiki/Bonde_Palace "Bonde Palace") in Stockholm, seat of the [Supreme Court of Sweden](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Sweden "Supreme Court of Sweden")

The [Supreme Court of Sweden](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Sweden "Supreme Court of Sweden") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Högsta domstolen*) is the third and final instance in all civil and criminal cases in Sweden. The Supreme Court consists of 16 Justices ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *justitieråd*), appointed by the Government, but the court as an institution is independent of the Riksdag, and the Government is not able to interfere with the decisions of the court.

According to a victimisation survey of 1,201 residents in 2005, Sweden has above-average [crime rates](/wiki/Crime_rate "Crime rate") compared to other EU countries. Sweden has high or above-average levels of assaults, sexual assaults, hate crimes, and consumer fraud. Sweden has low levels of burglary, car theft and drug problems. Bribe seeking is rare.[[173]](#cite_note-burdenofcrime2005-183) A mid-November 2013 news report announced that four prisons in Sweden were closed during the year due to a significant drop in the number of inmates, with prison numbers in Sweden falling by around 1% a year since 2004.[[174]](#cite_note-184)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Sweden](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Sweden "Foreign relations of Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/P20230705AS-0417_%2853233830047%29.jpg/250px-P20230705AS-0417_%2853233830047%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:P20230705AS-0417_(53233830047).jpg)

Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson with U.S. President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), 2023

Throughout the 20th century, [Swedish foreign policy](/wiki/Swedish_foreign_policy "Swedish foreign policy") was based on the principle of non-alignment in peacetime and [neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country") in wartime. Sweden's government pursued an independent course of nonalignment in times of peace so that neutrality would be possible in the event of war.[[92]](#cite_note-NB335-339-100)

During the early Cold War era, Sweden combined its policy of non-alignment and a low profile in international affairs with a [security policy](/wiki/Security_policy "Security policy") based on strong [national defence](/wiki/National_security "National security").[[175]](#cite_note-185) The function of the Swedish military was to deter attack.[[176]](#cite_note-186) Beginning in the late 1960s, Sweden attempted to play a more significant and independent role in international relations. It involved itself significantly in international peace efforts, especially through the United Nations, and in support of the [Third World](/wiki/Third_World "Third World"). Following the [1986 assassination of Olof Palme](/wiki/Assassination_of_Olof_Palme "Assassination of Olof Palme") and with the end of the Cold War, Sweden has adopted a more traditional foreign policy approach. Nevertheless, the country remains active in peacekeeping missions and maintains a considerable foreign aid budget.

Since 1995 Sweden has been a member of the European Union, and as a consequence of a new world security situation the country's foreign policy doctrine has been partly modified, with Sweden playing a more active role in European security co-operation. In 2022, in response to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, Sweden moved to formally join the NATO alliance.[[103]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-111) Sweden formally became a member of NATO in 2024.[[106]](#cite_note-NATO_Sweden_Accession_Protocol-114)

### Military

Main articles: [Swedish Armed Forces](/wiki/Swedish_Armed_Forces "Swedish Armed Forces") and [Law enforcement in Sweden](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Sweden "Law enforcement in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Saab_JAS_39_Gripen_at_Kaivopuisto_Air_Show%2C_June_2017_%28altered%29_copy.jpg/250px-Saab_JAS_39_Gripen_at_Kaivopuisto_Air_Show%2C_June_2017_%28altered%29_copy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saab_JAS_39_Gripen_at_Kaivopuisto_Air_Show,_June_2017_(altered)_copy.jpg)

The [Saab](/wiki/Saab_AB "Saab AB") [JAS 39 Gripen](/wiki/JAS_39_Gripen "JAS 39 Gripen") is an advanced Swedish multi-role [fighter aircraft](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft") of the [Swedish Air Force](/wiki/Swedish_Air_Force "Swedish Air Force").

The [law is enforced in Sweden](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Sweden "Law enforcement in Sweden") by several government entities. The Swedish police is a [Government agency](/wiki/Government_agencies_in_Sweden "Government agencies in Sweden") concerned with police matters. The [National Task Force](/wiki/National_Task_Force "National Task Force") is a national [SWAT](/wiki/SWAT "SWAT") unit within the police force. The [Swedish Security Service](/wiki/Swedish_Security_Service "Swedish Security Service")'s responsibilities are [counter-espionage](/wiki/Counter-espionage "Counter-espionage"), anti-terrorist activities, protection of the constitution and protection of sensitive objects and people.

The *Försvarsmakten* (Swedish Armed Forces) are a government agency reporting to the Swedish [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Sweden) "Ministry of Defence (Sweden)") and responsible for the [peacetime](/wiki/Peacetime "Peacetime") operation of the armed forces of Sweden. The primary task of the agency is to train and deploy peacekeeping forces abroad, while maintaining the long-term ability to refocus on the defence of Sweden in the event of war. The armed forces are divided into [Army](/wiki/Swedish_Army "Swedish Army"), [Air Force](/wiki/Swedish_Air_Force "Swedish Air Force") and [Navy](/wiki/Swedish_Navy "Swedish Navy"). The head of the armed forces is the [Supreme Commander](/wiki/Supreme_Commander_of_the_Swedish_Armed_Forces "Supreme Commander of the Swedish Armed Forces") (*Överbefälhavaren*, ÖB), the most senior commissioned officer in the country. Up to 1974, the King was *pro forma* [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander_in_chief#Sweden "Commander in chief"), but in reality it was clearly understood through the 20th century that the monarch would have no *active* role as a military leader.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Stridsfordon_90_Revinge_2012-2.jpg/250px-Stridsfordon_90_Revinge_2012-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stridsfordon_90_Revinge_2012-2.jpg)

The Infantry fighting vehicle [CV90](/wiki/Combat_Vehicle_90 "Combat Vehicle 90"), which is produced and used by Sweden

Until the end of the Cold War, nearly all males reaching the age of [military service](/wiki/Military_service "Military service") were [conscripted](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription"). In recent years, the number of male conscripts has declined markedly, whilst the number of female volunteers has seen a modest rise. Recruitment has increasingly focused on attracting the most committed candidates. Under current legislation, all personnel deployed abroad are required to be volunteers. As of 1 July 2010, Sweden discontinued regular conscription, moving to an all-volunteer military, except where compulsory service may be reinstated for national defence purposes.[[177]](#cite_note-187)[[178]](#cite_note-188)[[179]](#cite_note-189) The total forces gathered would consist of about 60,000 personnel. This in comparison with the 1980s, before the fall of the Soviet Union, when Sweden could gather up to 1,000,000 service members.

However, on 11 December 2014, due to tensions in the Baltic area, the [Swedish Government](/wiki/Swedish_Government "Swedish Government") reintroduced one part of the [Swedish conscription system](/wiki/Conscription_in_Sweden "Conscription in Sweden"), [refresher training](/wiki/Refresher_training_(military) "Refresher training (military)").[[180]](#cite_note-190) On 2 March 2017, the government decided to reintroduce the remaining part of the Swedish conscription system, basic military training. The first recruits began their training in 2018. As the law is now gender neutral, both men and women may have to serve.[[181]](#cite_note-191)

Sweden decided not to sign the UN treaty on the [Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[182]](#cite_note-192) Swedish units have taken part in peacekeeping operations in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Cyprus, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Kosovo, Liberia, Lebanon, Afghanistan and Chad.[[183]](#cite_note-193)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Sweden](/wiki/Economy_of_Sweden "Economy of Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Volvo_EX90_Auto_Zuerich_2024_DSC_6147.jpg/250px-Volvo_EX90_Auto_Zuerich_2024_DSC_6147.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volvo_EX90_Auto_Zuerich_2024_DSC_6147.jpg)

Sweden is home to [Volvo Cars](/wiki/Volvo_Cars "Volvo Cars"), an automobile company with its headquarters in [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/IKEA_%288020223012%29.jpg/250px-IKEA_%288020223012%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:IKEA_(8020223012).jpg)

[IKEA](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA"), a Swedish multinational conglomerate, is the world's largest furniture retailer

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/H%26M_Canal_City_Hakata_shop.JPG/250px-H%26M_Canal_City_Hakata_shop.JPG)](/wiki/File:H%26M_Canal_City_Hakata_shop.JPG)

[H&M](/wiki/H%26M "H&M"), one of the world's largest fashion retailers

Sweden is the twelfth-richest country in the world in terms of GDP (gross domestic product) per capita and a high [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living") is experienced by its citizens. Sweden is an export-oriented [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy"). Timber, [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower") and iron ore constitute the resource base of an economy with a heavy emphasis on [foreign trade](/wiki/Foreign_trade "Foreign trade"). Sweden's engineering sector accounts for 50% of output and exports, while telecommunications, the automotive industry and the pharmaceutical industries are also of great importance. Sweden is the ninth-largest [arms exporter in the world](/wiki/Arms_industry#World's_largest_arms_exporters "Arms industry"). [Agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Sweden "Agriculture in Sweden") accounts for 2% of GDP and employment. The country ranks among the highest for telephone and Internet access penetration.[[184]](#cite_note-194)

Trade unions, employers' associations and collective agreements cover a large share of the employees in Sweden.[[185]](#cite_note-195)[[186]](#cite_note-196) The high coverage of collective agreements is achieved despite the absence of state mechanisms extending collective agreements to whole industries or sectors. Both the prominent role of collective bargaining and the way in which the high rate of coverage is achieved reflect the dominance of self-regulation (regulation by the labour market parties themselves).[[187]](#cite_note-197) When the Swedish [Ghent system](/wiki/Ghent_system "Ghent system") was changed in 2007, resulting in considerably raised fees to unemployment funds, a substantial decline in union density and density of unemployment funds occurred.[[188]](#cite_note-198)[[189]](#cite_note-199)

In 2010, Sweden's income [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient") was the third lowest among developed countries, at 0.25—slightly higher than Japan and Denmark—suggesting [Sweden had low income inequality](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_Sweden "Income inequality in Sweden"). However, Sweden's wealth Gini coefficient at 0.853 was the second highest in developed countries, and above European and North American averages, suggesting high wealth inequality.[[190]](#cite_note-hdr2010-200)[[191]](#cite_note-cs2010-201) Even on a disposable income basis, the geographical distribution of Gini coefficient of income inequality varies within different regions and municipalities of Sweden. [Danderyd](/wiki/Danderyd_Municipality "Danderyd Municipality"), outside Stockholm, has Sweden's highest Gini coefficient of income inequality, at 0.55, while [Hofors](/wiki/Hofors_Municipality "Hofors Municipality") near Gävle has the lowest at 0.25. In and around Stockholm and Scania, two of the more densely populated regions of Sweden, the income Gini coefficient is between 0.35 and 0.55.[[192]](#cite_note-202)

In terms of structure, the Swedish economy is characterised by a large, knowledge-intensive and export-oriented manufacturing sector; an increasing, but comparatively small, business [service sector](/wiki/Service_sector "Service sector"); and by international standards, a large public service sector. Large organisations, both in manufacturing and services, dominate the Swedish economy.[[193]](#cite_note-203) High and medium-high technology manufacturing accounts for 9.9% of GDP.[[194]](#cite_note-204)

The 20 largest (by turnover) registered Swedish companies in 2007 were [Volvo](/wiki/Volvo "Volvo"), [Ericsson](/wiki/Ericsson "Ericsson"), [Vattenfall](/wiki/Vattenfall "Vattenfall"), [Skanska](/wiki/Skanska "Skanska"), [Sony Ericsson Mobile Communications AB](/wiki/Sony_Ericsson_Mobile_Communications_AB "Sony Ericsson Mobile Communications AB"), [Svenska Cellulosa Aktiebolaget](/wiki/Svenska_Cellulosa_Aktiebolaget "Svenska Cellulosa Aktiebolaget"), [Electrolux](/wiki/Electrolux "Electrolux"), [Volvo Personvagnar](/wiki/Volvo_Personvagnar "Volvo Personvagnar"), [TeliaSonera](/wiki/TeliaSonera "TeliaSonera"), [Sandvik](/wiki/Sandvik "Sandvik"), [Scania](/wiki/Scania_AB "Scania AB"), [ICA](/wiki/ICA_AB "ICA AB"), [Hennes & Mauritz](/wiki/Hennes_%26_Mauritz "Hennes & Mauritz"), [IKEA](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA"), [Nordea](/wiki/Nordea "Nordea"), [Preem](/wiki/Preem "Preem"), [Atlas Copco](/wiki/Atlas_Copco "Atlas Copco"), [Securitas](/wiki/Securitas_AB "Securitas AB"), [Nordstjernan](/wiki/Nordstjernan "Nordstjernan") and [SKF](/wiki/SKF "SKF").[[195]](#cite_note-205) The vast majority of Sweden's industry is [privately](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") controlled, unlike many other industrialised Western countries.

An estimated 4.5 million Swedish residents are employed, and around a half of the workforce completed tertiary education.[[196]](#cite_note-206) In terms of GDP per-hour-worked, Sweden was the world's ninth highest in 2006 at US$31, compared to US$22 in Spain and US$35 in the United States.[[197]](#cite_note-oecd2007-207) GDP per-hour-worked is growing 2.5% per year for the economy as a whole and the trade-terms-balanced productivity growth is 2%.[[197]](#cite_note-oecd2007-207) According to the OECD, deregulation, globalisation, and technology sector growth have been key productivity drivers.[[197]](#cite_note-oecd2007-207) Sweden is a world leader in privatised pensions and pension funding problems are relatively small compared to many other Western European countries.[[198]](#cite_note-208) A pilot program to test the feasibility of a six-hour workday, without loss of pay, will commence in 2014, involving the participation of Gothenburg municipal staff. The Swedish government is seeking to reduce its costs through decreased sick leave hours and increased efficiency.[[199]](#cite_note-209)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Spotify_App_%2823745887894%29.jpg/250px-Spotify_App_%2823745887894%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spotify_App_(23745887894).jpg)

[Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), Swedish audio streaming service with more than 700 million users

The typical worker receives 40% of their labour costs after the [tax wedge](/wiki/Tax_wedge "Tax wedge"). Total tax collected by Sweden as a percentage of its GDP peaked at 52.3% in 1990.[[200]](#cite_note-oecdtax11-210) The country faced a real estate and banking crisis in 1990–1991, and consequently passed tax reforms in 1991 to implement tax rate cuts and tax base broadening over time.[[201]](#cite_note-AgellEnglund-211)[[202]](#cite_note-RiksbankHeikensten-212) Since 1990, taxes as a percentage of GDP collected by Sweden have been dropping, with total tax rates for the highest income earners dropping the most.[[203]](#cite_note-BengtssonHolmlund-213) In 2010 45.8% of the country's GDP was collected as taxes, the second highest among OECD countries, and nearly double the percentage in the US or South Korea.[[200]](#cite_note-oecdtax11-210) Tax income-financed employment represents a third of the Swedish workforce, a substantially higher proportion than in most other countries. Overall, GDP growth has been fast since reforms—especially those in manufacturing—were enacted in the early 1990s.[[204]](#cite_note-oecd2005-214)

Sweden is the fourth-most competitive economy in the world, according to the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") in its *[Global Competitiveness Report](/wiki/Global_Competitiveness_Report "Global Competitiveness Report") 2012–2013*.[[15]](#cite_note-wefcomp-23) Sweden is the top performing country in the 2014 *Global Green Economy Index (GGEI)*.[[205]](#cite_note-ggei-215) Sweden is ranked sixth in the IMD World Competitiveness Yearbook 2024.[[206]](#cite_note-imd-216)

Sweden maintains its own currency, the [Swedish krona](/wiki/Swedish_krona "Swedish krona") (SEK). The Swedish [Riksbank](/wiki/Sveriges_Riksbank "Sveriges Riksbank")—founded in 1668 and thus the oldest central bank in the world—is currently focusing on price stability with an inflation target of 2%. According to the *Economic Survey of Sweden 2007* by the OECD, the average inflation in Sweden has been one of the lowest among European countries since the mid-1990s, largely because of deregulation and quick utilisation of globalisation.[[197]](#cite_note-oecd2007-207)

The principal trading partners are Germany, the United States, Norway, the United Kingdom, Denmark, and Finland.

### Energy

See also: [Energy in Sweden](/wiki/Energy_in_Sweden "Energy in Sweden"), [Electricity in Sweden](/wiki/Electricity_in_Sweden "Electricity in Sweden"), [Nordic energy market](/wiki/Nordic_energy_market "Nordic energy market"), and [Nuclear power in Sweden](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_Sweden "Nuclear power in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Ringhals.JPG/250px-Ringhals.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ringhals.JPG)

[Ringhals Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Ringhals_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Ringhals Nuclear Power Plant"), located south of [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg")

Sweden's energy market is largely privatised. The [Nordic energy market](/wiki/Nordic_energy_market "Nordic energy market") is one of the first liberalised energy markets in Europe and it is traded in [NASDAQ OMX Commodities Europe](/wiki/NASDAQ_OMX_Commodities_Europe "NASDAQ OMX Commodities Europe") and [Nord Pool Spot](/wiki/Nord_Pool_Spot "Nord Pool Spot"). In 2006, out of a total electricity production of 139 [TWh](/wiki/TWh "TWh"), electricity from hydropower accounted for 61 TWh (44%), and [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") delivered 65 TWh (47%). At the same time, the use of [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel"), [peat](/wiki/Peat "Peat") etc. produced 13 TWh (9%) of electricity, while wind power produced 1 TWh (1%). Sweden was a net importer of electricity by a margin of 6 TWh.[[207]](#cite_note-217) [Biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") is mainly used to produce heat for [district heating](/wiki/District_heating "District heating") and [central heating](/wiki/Central_heating "Central heating") and industry processes.

Sweden joined the [International Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") in 1974, after the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") strengthened Sweden's commitment to decrease dependence on imported fossil fuels.[[208]](#cite_note-:02-218) To protect against unexpected oil supply shocks and in accordance with international commitments made through the IEA, Sweden maintains a strategic petroleum reserve of at least 90 days of net oil imports. As of February 2022, Sweden's [oil reserves](/wiki/Oil_reserves "Oil reserves") totalled 130 days' worth of net imports.[[209]](#cite_note-219) Sweden has moved to generate electricity mostly from hydropower and nuclear power. The use of nuclear power has been limited, however. Among other things, the accident of [Three Mile Island](/wiki/Three_Mile_Island "Three Mile Island") prompted the Riksdag to ban new nuclear plants. In March 2005, an opinion poll showed that 83% supported maintaining or increasing nuclear power.[[210]](#cite_note-220)

Sweden is considered a "global leader" in [decarbonisation](/wiki/Decarbonisation "Decarbonisation").[[208]](#cite_note-:02-218) Politicians have made announcements about oil phase-out in Sweden, decrease of nuclear power, and multibillion-dollar investments in [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") and energy efficiency.[[211]](#cite_note-Agenda212-221)[[212]](#cite_note-Vidal2-222) The country has for many years pursued a strategy of indirect taxation as an instrument of [environmental policy](/wiki/Environmental_policy "Environmental policy"), including [energy taxes](/wiki/Energy_tax "Energy tax") in general and [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") taxes in particular.[[211]](#cite_note-Agenda212-221) Sweden was the first nation to implement [carbon pricing](/wiki/Carbon_price "Carbon price"), and its carbon prices remain the highest in the world as of 2020. This model has been shown to be particularly effective at decarbonizing the nation's economy.[[208]](#cite_note-:02-218)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Sweden](/wiki/Transport_in_Sweden "Transport in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/%C3%98resund_Bridge_from_the_air_in_September_2015.jpg/250px-%C3%98resund_Bridge_from_the_air_in_September_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%98resund_Bridge_from_the_air_in_September_2015.jpg)

The [Öresund Bridge](/wiki/%C3%96resund_Bridge "Öresund Bridge") between [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö") and [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen") in Denmark

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/E6-E20_Olskroksmotet_in_Gothenburg.png/250px-E6-E20_Olskroksmotet_in_Gothenburg.png)](/wiki/File:E6-E20_Olskroksmotet_in_Gothenburg.png)

The west coast motorway [E6](/wiki/European_route_E6 "European route E6")/[E20](/wiki/European_route_E20 "European route E20") in central [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg")

Sweden has 162,707 km (101,101 mi) of paved road and 1,428 km (887 mi) of expressways. [Motorways](/wiki/List_of_motorways_in_Sweden "List of motorways in Sweden") run through Sweden and over the Öresund Bridge to Denmark. Sweden had left-hand traffic (*vänstertrafik* in Swedish) from approximately 1736, but after the Riksdag passed legislation in 1963 changeover took place on 3 September 1967, known in Swedish as [Dagen H](/wiki/Dagen_H "Dagen H").

The [Stockholm metro](/wiki/Stockholm_metro "Stockholm metro") is the only underground system in Sweden and serves the city of Stockholm via 100 stations. The rail transport market is privatised, but while there are many privately owned enterprises, the largest operators are still owned by the state. Operators include [SJ](/wiki/SJ_AB "SJ AB"), [Veolia Transport](/wiki/Veolia_Transport "Veolia Transport"), [Green Cargo](/wiki/Green_Cargo "Green Cargo"), [Tågkompaniet](/wiki/T%C3%A5gkompaniet "Tågkompaniet") and [Inlandsbanan](/wiki/Inlandsbanan "Inlandsbanan"). Most of the railways are owned and operated by [Trafikverket](/wiki/Trafikverket "Trafikverket").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Hall%2C_Stockholm_Central_Station.jpg/250px-Hall%2C_Stockholm_Central_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hall,_Stockholm_Central_Station.jpg)

[Stockholm Central Station](/wiki/Stockholm_Central_Station "Stockholm Central Station")

Most tram networks were closed in 1967. But they survived in [Norrköping](/wiki/Norrk%C3%B6ping "Norrköping"), Stockholm and Gothenburg, with [Gothenburg tram network](/wiki/Gothenburg_tram_network "Gothenburg tram network") being the largest. [A new tram line opened](/wiki/Lund_tramway "Lund tramway") in [Lund](/wiki/Lund "Lund") on 13 December 2020.

The largest airports include [Stockholm–Arlanda Airport](/wiki/Stockholm%E2%80%93Arlanda_Airport "Stockholm–Arlanda Airport") (16.1 million passengers in 2009) 40 km (25 mi) north of Stockholm, [Göteborg Landvetter Airport](/wiki/G%C3%B6teborg_Landvetter_Airport "Göteborg Landvetter Airport") (4.3 million passengers in 2008), and [Stockholm–Skavsta Airport](/wiki/Stockholm%E2%80%93Skavsta_Airport "Stockholm–Skavsta Airport") (2.0 million passengers). Sweden hosts the two largest port companies in Scandinavia, [Port of Göteborg AB](/wiki/Port_of_Gothenburg "Port of Gothenburg") (Gothenburg) and the transnational company [Copenhagen Malmö Port AB](/wiki/Copenhagen_Malm%C3%B6_Port "Copenhagen Malmö Port"). [Malmö Airport](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6_Airport "Malmö Airport") in Scania, southern Sweden, is Sweden's [fifth-busiest airport](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_the_Nordic_countries "List of the busiest airports in the Nordic countries").

Sweden also has a number of car ferry connections to several neighbouring countries.[[213]](#cite_note-223) This includes a route from [Umeå](/wiki/Ume%C3%A5 "Umeå") across [the Gulf of Bothnia](/wiki/Kvarken "Kvarken") to [Vaasa](/wiki/Vaasa "Vaasa") in Finland. There are several connections from the Stockholm area across the [Sea of Åland](/wiki/Sea_of_%C3%85land "Sea of Åland") to [Mariehamn](/wiki/Mariehamn "Mariehamn") in [Åland](/wiki/%C3%85land "Åland") as well as [Turku](/wiki/Turku "Turku") and [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki") on the Finnish mainland and beyond to Estonia and [St Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg") in Russia. Ferry routes from the Stockholm area also connect with Latvia and Poland across the Baltic Sea. The ferry ports of [Karlskrona](/wiki/Karlskrona "Karlskrona") and [Karlshamn](/wiki/Karlshamn "Karlshamn") in southeastern Sweden serve Poland and Lithuania. Ystad and Trelleborg near the southern tip of Sweden have ferry links with the Danish island of [Bornholm](/wiki/Bornholm "Bornholm") and the German ports of [Sassnitz](/wiki/Sassnitz "Sassnitz"), [Rostock](/wiki/Rostock "Rostock") and [Travemünde](/wiki/Travem%C3%BCnde "Travemünde"), respectively, and ferries run to [Świnoujście](/wiki/%C5%9Awinouj%C5%9Bcie "Świnoujście"), Poland, from both of them. Trelleborg is the busiest ferry port in Sweden in terms of weight transported by lorry.[[214]](#cite_note-224) Despite the opening of the fixed link to Denmark, the [Öresund Bridge](/wiki/%C3%96resund_Bridge "Öresund Bridge"), the busiest ferry route remains the short link across the narrowest section of the [Öresund](/wiki/%C3%96resund "Öresund") between [Helsingborg](/wiki/Helsingborg "Helsingborg") and the Danish port of [Helsingør](/wiki/Helsing%C3%B8r "Helsingør"), known as the [HH Ferry route](/wiki/HH_Ferry_route "HH Ferry route"). There are over seventy departures a day each way; during peak times, a ferry departs every fifteen minutes.[[215]](#cite_note-225) Ports higher up the Swedish west coast include [Varberg](/wiki/Varberg "Varberg"), with a ferry connection across the [Kattegat](/wiki/Kattegat "Kattegat") to [Grenaa](/wiki/Grenaa "Grenaa") in Denmark, and Gothenburg, serving [Frederikshavn](/wiki/Frederikshavn "Frederikshavn") at the northern tip of Denmark and [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel") in Germany. Finally, there are ferries from [Strömstad](/wiki/Str%C3%B6mstad "Strömstad") near the Norwegian border to destinations around the [Oslofjord](/wiki/Oslofjord "Oslofjord") in Norway.

Sweden operates two domestic ferry routes using large vessels, both of which connect Gotland with the mainland. The lines leave from Visby harbour on the island, and the ferries sail to either [Oskarshamn](/wiki/Oskarshamn "Oskarshamn") or Nynäshamn.[[216]](#cite_note-226) A smaller car ferry connects the island of [Ven](/wiki/Ven_(Sweden) "Ven (Sweden)") in Öresund with [Landskrona](/wiki/Landskrona "Landskrona").[[217]](#cite_note-227)

### Public policy

See also: [Nordic model](/wiki/Nordic_model "Nordic model") and [Social welfare in Sweden](/wiki/Social_welfare_in_Sweden "Social welfare in Sweden")

Sweden has one of the most highly developed welfare states in the world. According to a 2012 OECD report, the country had the second-highest public social spending as a percentage of its GDP after France, and the third-highest total (public and private) social spending at 30.2% of its GDP, after France and Belgium.[[218]](#cite_note-228) Sweden spent 6.3% of its GDP, the ninth-highest among 34 OECD countries, to provide equal access to education.[[219]](#cite_note-229) On health care, the country spent 10.0% of its total GDP, the 12th highest.[[220]](#cite_note-230)

Historically, Sweden provided solid support for [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") (except agriculture) and mostly relatively strong and stable property rights (both private and public), though some economists have pointed out that Sweden promoted industries with tariffs and used publicly subsidised R&D during the country's early critical years of industrialisation.[[221]](#cite_note-H-J.C-Sweden-231) After World War II a succession of governments expanded the welfare state by raising the taxes. A series of successive social reforms transformed the country into one of the most equal and developed on earth. The consistent growth of the welfare state led to Swedes achieving unprecedented levels of social mobility and quality of life—to this day Sweden consistently ranks at the top of league tables for health, literacy and Human Development—far ahead of some wealthier countries (for example the United States).[[222]](#cite_note-Equal_Societies-232) A report from the [United Nations Development Program](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Program "United Nations Development Program") predicted that Sweden's rating on the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") will fall from 0.949 in 2010 to 0.906 in 2030.[[223]](#cite_note-233)

Sweden has been relatively quick to adopt [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") policies, such as [privatisation](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation"), [financialisation](/wiki/Financialisation "Financialisation") and [deregulation](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation"),[[224]](#cite_note-234)[[225]](#cite_note-235) compared to countries such as France.[[197]](#cite_note-oecd2007-207)[[226]](#cite_note-236) The current Swedish government is continuing the trend of moderate rollbacks of previous social reforms.[[197]](#cite_note-oecd2007-207)[[227]](#cite_note-237) Growth has been higher than in many other [EU-15](/wiki/EU-15 "EU-15") countries. Since the mid-1980s, Sweden has had the fastest growth in inequality of any developed nation, according to the OECD. This has largely been attributed to the reduction in state benefits and a shift toward the privatisation of public services. Nevertheless, it remains far more egalitarian than most nations.[[110]](#cite_note-nytimes-118)[[228]](#cite_note-238)

Sweden adopted free market agricultural policies in 1990. Since the 1930s, the agricultural sector had been subject to price controls. In June 1990, the Riksdag voted for a new agricultural policy marking a significant shift away from price controls. As a result, [food prices](/wiki/Food_prices "Food prices") fell somewhat. However, the liberalisations soon became moot because EU agricultural controls supervened.[[229]](#cite_note-239)

In 2015 and 2016, 69 per cent of the employed workers is organised in trade unions. Union density in 2016 was 62% among blue-collar-workers (most of them in the [Swedish Trade Union Confederation](/wiki/Swedish_Trade_Union_Confederation "Swedish Trade Union Confederation"), LO) and 75% among white-collar workers (most of them in the Swedish Confederation of Professional Employees, TCO, and the [Swedish Confederation of Professional Associations](/wiki/Swedish_Confederation_of_Professional_Associations "Swedish Confederation of Professional Associations"), SACO).[[230]](#cite_note-240) Sweden has state-supported union unemployment funds ([Ghent system](/wiki/Ghent_system "Ghent system")).[[231]](#cite_note-241) Trade unions have the right to elect two representatives to the board in all Swedish companies with more than 25 employees. Sweden has a relatively high amount of sick leave per worker in OECD: the average worker loses 24 days due to sickness.[[204]](#cite_note-oecd2005-214)

The unemployment rate was 7.2% in May 2017 while the employment rate was 67.4%, with the workforce consisting of 4,983,000 people while 387,000 are unemployed.[[232]](#cite_note-242)[[233]](#cite_note-243) Unemployment among youth (aged 24 or younger) in 2012 was 24.2%, making Sweden the OECD country with the highest ratio of youth unemployment versus unemployment in general.[[234]](#cite_note-244)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Swedish inventions](/wiki/Swedish_inventions "Swedish inventions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/AlfredNobel_adjusted.jpg/250px-AlfredNobel_adjusted.jpg)](/wiki/File:AlfredNobel_adjusted.jpg)

[Alfred Nobel](/wiki/Alfred_Nobel "Alfred Nobel"), inventor of dynamite and institutor of the Nobel Prize

In the 18th century, Sweden's [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") took off. Previously, technical progress had mainly come from mainland Europe.

In 1739, the [Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Royal_Swedish_Academy_of_Sciences "Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences") was founded, with people such as [Carl Linnaeus](/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus "Carl Linnaeus") and [Anders Celsius](/wiki/Anders_Celsius "Anders Celsius") as early members. [Gustaf Dalén](/wiki/Gustaf_Dal%C3%A9n "Gustaf Dalén") founded [AGA](/wiki/AGA_AB "AGA AB"), and received the Nobel Prize for his [sun valve](/wiki/Sun_valve "Sun valve"). [Alfred Nobel](/wiki/Alfred_Nobel "Alfred Nobel") invented [dynamite](/wiki/Dynamite "Dynamite") and instituted the Nobel Prizes. [Lars Magnus Ericsson](/wiki/Lars_Magnus_Ericsson "Lars Magnus Ericsson") started the company bearing his name, Ericsson, still one of the largest telecom companies in the world. [Jonas Wenström](/wiki/Jonas_Wenstr%C3%B6m "Jonas Wenström") was an early pioneer in [alternating current](/wiki/Alternating_current "Alternating current") and is along with [Nikola Tesla](/wiki/Nikola_Tesla "Nikola Tesla") credited as one of the inventors of the three-phase electrical system.[[235]](#cite_note-si91e-245)

The traditional engineering industry is still a major source of Swedish inventions, but pharmaceuticals, electronics and other high-tech industries are gaining ground. [Tetra Pak](/wiki/Tetra_Pak "Tetra Pak") was an invention for storing liquid foods, invented by [Erik Wallenberg](/wiki/Erik_Wallenberg "Erik Wallenberg"). [Losec](/wiki/Losec "Losec"), an ulcer medicine, was the world's best-selling drug in the 1990s and was developed by [AstraZeneca](/wiki/AstraZeneca "AstraZeneca"). More recently [Håkan Lans](/wiki/H%C3%A5kan_Lans "Håkan Lans") invented the [Automatic Identification System](/wiki/Automatic_Identification_System "Automatic Identification System"), a worldwide standard for shipping and civil aviation navigation. A large portion of the Swedish economy is to this day based on the export of technical inventions.[[235]](#cite_note-si91e-245)

Swedish inventors held 47,112 patents in the United States in 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sweden&action=edit), according to the [United States Patent and Trademark Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office"). As a nation, only ten other countries hold more patents than Sweden.[[236]](#cite_note-246)

Combined, the public and the private sector in Sweden allocate over 3.5% of GDP to [research & development](/wiki/Research_%26_development "Research & development") (R&D) per year, making Sweden's investment in R&D as a percentage of GDP the second-highest in the world.[[237]](#cite_note-247) For several decades the [Swedish government](/wiki/Swedish_government "Swedish government") has prioritised scientific and R&D activities. As a percentage of GDP, the Swedish government spends the most of any nation on research and development.[[238]](#cite_note-248) Sweden tops other European countries in the number of published scientific works per capita.[[239]](#cite_note-249)

The [European Spallation Source](/wiki/European_Spallation_Source "European Spallation Source") (ESS) was scheduled to begin initial operations in 2019 with construction completion scheduled for 2025. The ESS will give an approximately 30 times stronger neutron beam than any of today's existing neutron source installations.[[240]](#cite_note-250) The [MAX IV](/wiki/MAX_IV "MAX IV"), costing some SEK 3 billion, was inaugurated on 21 June 2016. Both facilities have strong implications on material research. Sweden was ranked second in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2023, 2024 and 2025.[[241]](#cite_note-251)[[242]](#cite_note-252)[[243]](#cite_note-253)[[244]](#cite_note-254)[[245]](#cite_note-255)

### Waste management

Sweden is known for its efficient waste management system. Only 0.7% of the total household waste is disposed, and the rest is reused.[[246]](#cite_note-auto1-256) Around 52% of its waste is used for energy production (that is burnt) and 47% recycled.[[247]](#cite_note-257)[[248]](#cite_note-258) About two million tonnes of waste are imported from neighbouring countries to make profitable recycling products.[[246]](#cite_note-auto1-256) As of 2023 report, Sweden generated 1.7 billion euros in 2020 (the highest so far was 1.98 billion euros in 2016) from recycling waste.[[249]](#cite_note-259) The works are mostly executed through the public organisation, [Swedish Waste Management](/wiki/Swedish_Waste_Management "Swedish Waste Management") (Avfall Sverige).[[246]](#cite_note-auto1-256)

### Taxes

Main article: [Taxation in Sweden](/wiki/Taxation_in_Sweden "Taxation in Sweden")

Since the late 1960s, Sweden has had the highest tax quota (as percentage of GDP) in the industrialised world, although today the gap has narrowed and Denmark has surpassed Sweden as the most heavily taxed country among developed countries. Sweden has a two-step [progressive tax](/wiki/Progressive_tax "Progressive tax") scale with a municipal income tax of about 30% and an additional high-income state tax of 20–25% when a salary exceeds roughly 320,000 SEK per year. [Payroll taxes](/wiki/Payroll_tax "Payroll tax") amount to 32%. In addition, a national [VAT](/wiki/Value_added_tax "Value added tax") of 25% is added to many things bought by private citizens. Certain items are subject to additional taxes, e.g. electricity, petrol/diesel and alcoholic beverages. In 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sweden&action=edit), total tax revenue was 47.8% of GDP, the second-highest tax burden among developed countries, down from 49.1% 2006.[[250]](#cite_note-260) Public sector spending amounts to 53% of the GDP. State and municipal employees total around a third of the workforce, much more than in most Western countries. Only Denmark has a larger public sector (38% of Danish workforce). Spending on transfers is also high. On average, 27% of taxpayers' money in Sweden goes to education and healthcare, whereas 5% goes to the police and military, and 42% to social security.[[251]](#cite_note-261)

### Pensions

Main article: [Social security in Sweden](/wiki/Social_security_in_Sweden "Social security in Sweden")

Every Swedish resident receives a state pension. Swedish Pensions Agency is responsible for pensions. People who have worked in Sweden, but relocated to another country, can also receive the Swedish pension. There are several types of pensions in Sweden: occupational and private pensions, and national retirement. A person can receive a combination of the various types of pensions.
