The total resident population of Sweden was 10,588,020 in January 2025.[[252]](#cite_note-population-262) The population exceeded 10 million for the first time on Friday 20 January 2017.[[253]](#cite_note-263)[[254]](#cite_note-264)

The average population density is just over 25 people per km2 (65 per square mile), with 1 437 persons per km2 in localities (continuous settlement with at least 200 inhabitants).[[255]](#cite_note-SCB2020-265)[[256]](#cite_note-SCB2019-266) 88% of the population live in urban areas, which cover 1.5% of the entire land area.[[11]](#cite_note-SCBdensity2020-19)[[257]](#cite_note-SCB2018-267) 63% of Swedes are in large urban areas.[[257]](#cite_note-SCB2018-267) It is substantially higher in the south than in the north. There are more than 2000 localities.[[11]](#cite_note-SCBdensity2020-19) The capital city Stockholm has a municipal population of about 950,000 (with 1.5 million in the urban area and 2.3 million in the metropolitan area). The second- and third-largest cities are [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg") and [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö"). Outside of major cities, areas with notably higher population density include the agricultural part of Östergötland, the western coast, the area around [Lake Mälaren](/wiki/Lake_M%C3%A4laren "Lake Mälaren") and the agricultural area around Uppsala.

[Norrland](/wiki/Norrland "Norrland"), which covers approximately 60% of the Swedish territory, has a very low population density (below five people per square kilometre). The mountains and most of the remote coastal areas are almost unpopulated. Low population density exists also in large parts of western Svealand, as well as southern and central Småland. An area known as *[Finnveden](/wiki/Finnveden "Finnveden")*, which is located in the south-west of Småland, and mainly below the 57th parallel, can also be considered as almost empty of people.

There are no official statistics on ethnicity, but according to [Statistics Sweden](/wiki/Statistics_Sweden "Statistics Sweden"), 2,752,572 (26%) inhabitants of Sweden were of a [foreign background](/wiki/Immigration_to_Sweden "Immigration to Sweden") in 2021, defined as being born abroad or born in Sweden with both foreign-born parents.[[258]](#cite_note-:0-268) Of these inhabitants, 2,090,503 persons were born abroad and 662,069 persons were born in Sweden to parents born abroad. In addition, 805,340 persons had one parent born abroad with the other parent born in Sweden.[[258]](#cite_note-:0-268) Five minority groups are officially recognised by Sweden: [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Sweden "History of the Jews in Sweden"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people"), [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people"), [Sweden Finns](/wiki/Sweden_Finns "Sweden Finns"), and [Tornedalians](/wiki/Tornedalians "Tornedalians").[[259]](#cite_note-269)

Sweden has one of the oldest populations in the world, with the average age of 41.1 years.[[260]](#cite_note-270)

### Language

Main articles: [Swedish language](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") and [Languages of Sweden](/wiki/Languages_of_Sweden "Languages of Sweden")

See also: [Swedish dialects](/wiki/Swedish_dialects "Swedish dialects")

The official language of Sweden is [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"),[[1]](#cite_note-Swedish-2)[[2]](#cite_note-Swedish2-3) a North Germanic language, related and very similar to [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") and [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"), but differing in pronunciation and [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography"). [Sweden Finns](/wiki/Sweden_Finns "Sweden Finns") are Sweden's largest linguistic minority, comprising about 5% of Sweden's population,[[261]](#cite_note-Finns-271) and [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") is recognised as a minority language.[[2]](#cite_note-Swedish2-3) The [dialects spoken in Scania](/wiki/Scanian_dialects "Scanian dialects"), the southernmost part of the country, are influenced by Danish because the region was a part of Denmark until the treaty of Roskilde in 1658. Owing to a 21st-century influx of native speakers of [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), the use of Arabic is likely more widespread in the country than that of Finnish. However, no official statistics are kept on language use.[[262]](#cite_note-272)

Along with Finnish, [four other minority languages](/wiki/Minority_languages_of_Sweden "Minority languages of Sweden") are also recognised: [Meänkieli](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli "Meänkieli"), [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"), and [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"). Swedish became Sweden's official language on 1 July 2009, when a new language law was implemented.[[2]](#cite_note-Swedish2-3) The issue of whether Swedish should be declared the official language had been raised in the past, and the Riksdag voted on the matter in 2005, but the proposal narrowly failed.[[263]](#cite_note-273)

To varying degrees, a majority of Swedes, especially those born after World War II, understand and speak English, owing to trade links, the popularity of overseas travel, a strong Anglo-American influence and the tradition of [subtitling](/wiki/Subtitle_(captioning) "Subtitle (captioning)") rather than dubbing foreign television shows and films, and the [relative similarity](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") of the two languages which makes learning English easier. In a 2023 survey by [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer"), 90% of the population reported the ability to speak English at a conversational level.[[264]](#cite_note-EU-Lang-274) [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") is the second most widely spoken foreign language, with 25% reporting conversational proficiency, followed by [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") with 12%.[[264]](#cite_note-EU-Lang-274)

English became a compulsory subject for secondary school students studying [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science") as early as 1849, and has been a compulsory subject for all Swedish students since the late 1940s.[[265]](#cite_note-275) Most students also study one and sometimes two additional languages. Some Danish and Norwegian is also taught as part of Swedish courses for native speakers. Because of the extensive [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") between the three continental [Scandinavian languages](/wiki/Scandinavian_language "Scandinavian language"), Swedish speakers often use their native language when visiting or living in Norway or Denmark.

### Religion

Main articles: [Religion in Sweden](/wiki/Religion_in_Sweden "Religion in Sweden") and [Irreligion in Sweden](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Sweden "Irreligion in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Katarina_kyrka_February_2015_02.jpg/250px-Katarina_kyrka_February_2015_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katarina_kyrka_February_2015_02.jpg)

The Protestant [Katarina Church](/wiki/Katarina_Church "Katarina Church") in Stockholm

Before the 11th century, Swedes adhered to [Norse paganism](/wiki/Norse_paganism "Norse paganism"), worshipping [Æsir](/wiki/%C3%86sir "Æsir") gods, with its centre at the [Temple in Uppsala](/wiki/Temple_at_Uppsala "Temple at Uppsala"). With [Christianisation](/wiki/Christianization_of_Scandinavia "Christianization of Scandinavia") in the 11th century, the laws of the country changed, forbidding worship of other deities until the late 19th century. After the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") in the 1530s, the authority of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") was abolished and [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") became widespread. Adoption of Lutheranism was completed by the [Uppsala Synod](/wiki/Uppsala_Synod "Uppsala Synod") of 1593, and it became the official religion. During the era following the Reformation, usually known as the period of [Lutheran orthodoxy](/wiki/Lutheran_orthodoxy "Lutheran orthodoxy"), small groups of non-Lutherans, especially [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") Dutch, the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") and [French Huguenots](/wiki/French_Huguenots "French Huguenots") played a significant role in trade and industry, and were quietly tolerated.[[266]](#cite_note-276) The Sami originally had their own [shamanistic religion](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_shamanism "Sámi shamanism"), but they were converted to Lutheranism by Swedish missionaries in the 17th and 18th centuries.

With religious liberalisations in the late 18th century believers of other faiths, including [Judaism](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Sweden "History of the Jews in Sweden") and [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism"), were allowed to live and work freely in the country. However, until 1860 it remained illegal for Lutherans to convert to another religion. The 19th century saw the arrival of various [evangelical](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") [free churches](/wiki/Free_church "Free church"), and, towards the end of the century, [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism"), leading many to distance themselves from church rituals. Leaving the Church of Sweden became legal with the so-called [Dissenter Act](/wiki/Dissenter_Acts_(Sweden) "Dissenter Acts (Sweden)") of 1860, but only under the provision of entering another [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"). The right to stand outside any religious denomination was formally established in the law on [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") in 1951.

In 2000, the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden") was separated from the state. Sweden was the second [Nordic country](/wiki/Nordic_country "Nordic country") to [disestablish](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state") its [state church](/wiki/State_church "State church") (after Finland did so in the Church Act of 1869).[[267]](#cite_note-277)

At the end of 2024, 51.4% of Swedes belonged to the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden");[[268]](#cite_note-278) this number has been decreasing by 1-2 percentage points each year since 2001.[[269]](#cite_note-svenskakyrkan.se-279)[[270]](#cite_note-thelocal1-280)[[271]](#cite_note-281) Approximately 2% of the church's members regularly attend Sunday services.[[272]](#cite_note-282) The reason for the large number of inactive members is partly that, until 1996, children automatically became members at birth if at least one of the parents was a member. Since 1996, only children and adults who are [christened](/wiki/Infant_baptism "Infant baptism") become members. Some 275,000 Swedes are today members of various [Evangelical Protestant](/wiki/Evangelical_Protestant "Evangelical Protestant") free churches (where congregation attendance is much higher), and due to recent immigration, there are now some 100,000 [Eastern Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and 92,000 Roman Catholics living in Sweden.[[273]](#cite_note-283)

30% are ["none"](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") or "unspecified".[[274]](#cite_note-Britannica-284)

8% are "other" (than Church of Sweden).[[274]](#cite_note-Britannica-284)

The first Muslim congregation was established in 1949. Islam's presence in Sweden remained marginal until the 1960s, when Sweden started to receive migrants from [the Balkans](/wiki/The_Balkans "The Balkans") and Turkey. Further immigration from [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") have brought the estimated [Muslim population](/wiki/Islam_in_Sweden "Islam in Sweden") to 600,000.[[275]](#cite_note-285) However, only about 110,000 were members of a congregation around 2010.[[276]](#cite_note-286)[[277]](#cite_note-287)[[278]](#cite_note-288)

### Health

See also: [Healthcare in Sweden](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Sweden "Healthcare in Sweden") and [Swedish National Board of Health and Welfare](/wiki/Swedish_National_Board_of_Health_and_Welfare "Swedish National Board of Health and Welfare")

Healthcare in Sweden is mainly [tax-funded](/wiki/Publicly_funded_health_care "Publicly funded health care"), universal for all citizens, and decentralised,[[279]](#cite_note-289) although private health care also exists. The health care system in Sweden is financed primarily through taxes levied by regional councils and municipalities. A total of 21 councils are in charge of primary and hospital care within the country.

Private healthcare is uncommon in Sweden, and even private institutions operate under the supervision of local councils.[[280]](#cite_note-290) The city councils regulate the rules and the establishment of potential private practises. While care for the elderly or those who need psychiatric help is conducted privately in many other countries, in Sweden, publicly funded local authorities are in charge of this type of care.[[281]](#cite_note-291)

Healthcare in Sweden is similar in quality to other developed nations. Sweden ranks in the top five countries with respect to low [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality"). It also ranks high in [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") and in safe [drinking water](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water").[[282]](#cite_note-292) In 2018, health and medical care represented around 11% of GDP.[[283]](#cite_note-293)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Sweden](/wiki/Education_in_Sweden "Education in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Uppsala_University_2023.jpg/250px-Uppsala_University_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uppsala_University_2023.jpg)

[Uppsala University](/wiki/Uppsala_University "Uppsala University") (established 1477)

Children aged 1–5 years old are guaranteed a place in a public [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *förskola* or, colloquially, *dagis*). Between the ages of 6 and 16, children attend compulsory comprehensive school. In the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") (PISA), Swedish 15-year-old pupils score close to the OECD average.[[284]](#cite_note-294) After completing the ninth grade, about 90% of the students continue with a three-year upper secondary school (*gymnasium*), which can lead to both a job qualification or entrance eligibility to university. The school system is largely financed by taxes.

The Swedish government treats public and independent schools equally[[285]](#cite_note-swedishmodeleconomist-295) by introducing [education vouchers](/wiki/Education_voucher "Education voucher") in 1992 as one of the first countries in the world after the Netherlands. Anyone can establish a for-profit school and the municipality must pay new schools the same amount as municipal schools get. School lunch is free for all students in Sweden, and providing breakfast is also encouraged.[[286]](#cite_note-296)

There are a number of different [universities and colleges in Sweden](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Sweden "List of universities in Sweden"), the oldest and largest of which are situated in [Uppsala](/wiki/Uppsala_University "Uppsala University"), [Lund](/wiki/Lund_University "Lund University"), [Gothenburg](/wiki/University_of_Gothenburg "University of Gothenburg") and [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm_University "Stockholm University"). In 2000, 32% of Swedish people held a [tertiary degree](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education"), making the country fifth in the OECD in that category.[[287]](#cite_note-297) Along with several other European countries, the government also subsidises tuition of international students pursuing a degree at Swedish institutions, although a recent bill passed in the Riksdag will limit this subsidy to students from EEA countries and Switzerland.[[288]](#cite_note-298)

The large influx of immigrants to Swedish schools has been cited as a significant part of the reason why Sweden has dropped more than any other European country in the international [PISA](/wiki/PISA "PISA") rankings.[[289]](#cite_note-299)[[290]](#cite_note-300)[[291]](#cite_note-301)[[292]](#cite_note-302)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Sweden](/wiki/Culture_of_Sweden "Culture of Sweden")

Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, Sweden was seen as an international leader in what is now referred to as the "[sexual revolution](/wiki/Sexual_revolution "Sexual revolution")", with gender equality having particularly been promoted.[[293]](#cite_note-303)[[294]](#cite_note-304) Sweden has also become very liberal towards [homosexuality](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Sweden "LGBT rights in Sweden"), as is reflected in the popular acceptance of films such as *[Show Me Love](/wiki/Fucking_%C3%85m%C3%A5l "Fucking Åmål")*. Since 1 May 2009, Sweden repealed its "registered partnership" laws and fully replaced them with [gender-neutral marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[295]](#cite_note-305) Sweden also offers [domestic partnerships](/wiki/Domestic_partnership "Domestic partnership") for both same-sex and opposite-sex couples. Cohabitation (*sammanboende*) by couples of all ages, including teenagers as well as older couples, is widespread.

### Music

Main article: [Music of Sweden](/wiki/Music_of_Sweden "Music of Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/ABBA_-_TopPop_1974_5.png/250px-ABBA_-_TopPop_1974_5.png)](/wiki/File:ABBA_-_TopPop_1974_5.png)

The Swedish band [ABBA](/wiki/ABBA "ABBA") in April 1974, a few days after they won the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1974 "Eurovision Song Contest 1974")

Historical re-creations of Norse music have been attempted based on instruments found in Viking sites. The instruments used were the *[lur](/wiki/Lur "Lur")* (a sort of trumpet), simple string instruments, wooden flutes and drums. Sweden has a significant [folk-music](/wiki/Music_of_Sweden "Music of Sweden") scene. The *[joik](/wiki/Joik "Joik")*, a type of Sami music, is a chant that is part of the traditional Sami animistic spirituality. Notable composers include [Johan Helmich Roman](/wiki/Johan_Helmich_Roman "Johan Helmich Roman"), [Joseph Martin Kraus](/wiki/Joseph_Martin_Kraus "Joseph Martin Kraus"), [Franz Berwald](/wiki/Franz_Berwald "Franz Berwald"), [Wilhelm Stenhammar](/wiki/Wilhelm_Stenhammar "Wilhelm Stenhammar"), [Wilhelm Peterson-Berger](/wiki/Wilhelm_Peterson-Berger "Wilhelm Peterson-Berger"), [Hugo Alfvén](/wiki/Hugo_Alfv%C3%A9n "Hugo Alfvén"), [Kurt Atterberg](/wiki/Kurt_Atterberg "Kurt Atterberg"), [Ture Rangström](/wiki/Ture_Rangstr%C3%B6m "Ture Rangström"), [Hilding Rosenberg](/wiki/Hilding_Rosenberg "Hilding Rosenberg"), [Lars-Erik Larsson](/wiki/Lars-Erik_Larsson "Lars-Erik Larsson"), [Dag Wirén](/wiki/Dag_Wir%C3%A9n "Dag Wirén"), [Allan Pettersson](/wiki/Allan_Pettersson "Allan Pettersson"), [Karl-Birger Blomdahl](/wiki/Karl-Birger_Blomdahl "Karl-Birger Blomdahl"), [Ingvar Lidholm](/wiki/Ingvar_Lidholm "Ingvar Lidholm"), [Sven-David Sandström](/wiki/Sven-David_Sandstr%C3%B6m "Sven-David Sandström"), [Anders Eliasson](/wiki/Anders_Eliasson "Anders Eliasson") and [Anders Hillborg](/wiki/Anders_Hillborg "Anders Hillborg").

Sweden has a long tradition of "visor" – strophic songs with multiple verses telling different stories, often sung with lute or guitar. Notable singer/songwriters in this genre are [Carl-Michael Bellman](/wiki/Carl_Michael_Bellman "Carl Michael Bellman"), [Birger Sjöberg](/wiki/Birger_Sj%C3%B6berg "Birger Sjöberg"), [Evert Taube](/wiki/Evert_Taube "Evert Taube"), [Ulf Peder Olrog](/wiki/Ulf_Peder_Olrog "Ulf Peder Olrog"), [Olle Adolphson](/wiki/Olle_Adolphson "Olle Adolphson"), [Alf Hambe](/wiki/Alf_Hambe "Alf Hambe"), [Cornelis Vreeswijk](/wiki/Cornelis_Vreeswijk "Cornelis Vreeswijk"), [Stefan Sundström](/wiki/Stefan_Sundstr%C3%B6m "Stefan Sundström") and [Cajsa-Stina Åkerström.](/wiki/CajsaStina_%C3%85kerstr%C3%B6m "CajsaStina Åkerström")

Sweden also has a prominent choral music tradition. In 2003, it was estimated that five to six hundred thousand people sing in choirs.[[296]](#cite_note-306)

In 2007, with over 800 million dollars in revenue, Sweden was the third-largest music exporter in the world and surpassed only by the US and the UK.[[297]](#cite_note-export_music-307)[[298]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-308)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] According to one source in 2013, Sweden produces the most chart hits per capita in the world, followed by the UK and the US.[[299]](#cite_note-309)
Sweden has a rather lively [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") scene. The Centre for Swedish Folk Music and Jazz Research has published an overview of jazz in Sweden by Lars Westin.[[300]](#cite_note-310)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Sweden](/wiki/Architecture_of_Sweden "Architecture of Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/%C3%96stermalm%2C_Stockholm%2C_Sweden_-_panoramio_%2876%29.jpg/250px-%C3%96stermalm%2C_Stockholm%2C_Sweden_-_panoramio_%2876%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%96stermalm,_Stockholm,_Sweden_-_panoramio_(76).jpg)

[Djurgårdsbron](/wiki/Djurg%C3%A5rdsbron "Djurgårdsbron")

Before the 13th century, almost all buildings were made of timber, but a shift soon began towards stone. Early Swedish stone buildings are the [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") churches on the countryside. This would include the [Lund Cathedral](/wiki/Lund_Cathedral "Lund Cathedral") from the 11th century and the somewhat younger church in [Dalby](/wiki/Dalby,_Lund "Dalby, Lund"), but also many early [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_(architecture) "Gothic (architecture)") churches built through influences of the Hanseatic League, such as in Ystad, Malmö and Helsingborg.

Cathedrals in other parts of Sweden were also built as seats of Sweden's bishops. The [Skara Cathedral](/wiki/Skara_Cathedral "Skara Cathedral") is made of bricks from the 14th century, and the [Uppsala Cathedral](/wiki/Uppsala_Cathedral "Uppsala Cathedral") in the 15th century. In 1230, the foundations of the [Linköping](/wiki/Link%C3%B6ping "Linköping") Cathedral were made, the material was there [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), but the building took some 250 years to finish.

Among older structures are also some significant fortresses and other historical buildings such as at [Borgholm Castle](/wiki/Borgholm_Castle "Borgholm Castle"), [Halltorps Manor](/wiki/Halltorps_Manor "Halltorps Manor"), and [Eketorp](/wiki/Eketorp "Eketorp") fortress on the island Öland, the [Nyköping](/wiki/Nyk%C3%B6ping "Nyköping") fortress and the [Visby city wall](/wiki/Visby_city_wall "Visby city wall").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/00_1110_Kalmar_domkyrka.jpg/250px-00_1110_Kalmar_domkyrka.jpg)](/wiki/File:00_1110_Kalmar_domkyrka.jpg)

[Kalmar Cathedral](/wiki/Kalmar_Cathedral "Kalmar Cathedral")

In the 1520s, King [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_Vasa "Gustav Vasa") initiated grand mansions, castles, and fortresses to be built. Some of the more magnificent include [Kalmar Castle](/wiki/Kalmar_Castle "Kalmar Castle"), [Gripsholm Castle](/wiki/Gripsholm_Castle "Gripsholm Castle"), and [Vadstena](/wiki/Vadstena "Vadstena").

In the next two centuries, Sweden was designated by [Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") and later the [rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo"). Notable projects from that time include the city [Karlskrona](/wiki/Karlskrona "Karlskrona"), which has now also been declared a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") and the [Drottningholm Palace](/wiki/Drottningholm_Palace "Drottningholm Palace").

The [Stockholm exhibition](/wiki/Stockholm_Exhibition_(1930) "Stockholm Exhibition (1930)"), which marked the breakthrough of [Functionalism](/wiki/Functionalism_(architecture) "Functionalism (architecture)"), or *funkis* as it became known first surfaced in 1930; the style came to dominate in the following decades. Some notable projects of this kind were the [Million Programme](/wiki/Million_Programme "Million Programme"), offering affordable living in large apartment complexes.

The [Avicii Arena](/wiki/Avicii_Arena "Avicii Arena"), located in Stockholm, is the largest hemispherical building on Earth. Its dome has a diameter of 110 metres (360 feet) and took two and a half years to build.[[301]](#cite_note-311)

### Media

Main article: [Media in Sweden](/wiki/Media_in_Sweden "Media in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Sveriges_Television%2C_2.jpg/250px-Sveriges_Television%2C_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sveriges_Television,_2.jpg)

Headquarters of [Sveriges Television](/wiki/Sveriges_Television "Sveriges Television") in Stockholm

Swedes are among the greatest consumers of newspapers in the world, and nearly every town is served by a local paper. The country's main quality morning papers are *[Dagens Nyheter](/wiki/Dagens_Nyheter "Dagens Nyheter")* (liberal), *[Göteborgs-Posten](/wiki/G%C3%B6teborgs-Posten "Göteborgs-Posten")* (liberal), *[Svenska Dagbladet](/wiki/Svenska_Dagbladet "Svenska Dagbladet")* (liberal conservative) and *[Sydsvenska Dagbladet](/wiki/Sydsvenska_Dagbladet "Sydsvenska Dagbladet")* (liberal). The two largest evening [tabloids](/wiki/Tabloid_(newspaper_format) "Tabloid (newspaper format)") are *[Aftonbladet](/wiki/Aftonbladet "Aftonbladet")* (social democratic) and *[Expressen](/wiki/Expressen "Expressen")* (liberal). The ad-financed, free international morning paper, *[Metro International](/wiki/Metro_International "Metro International")*, was founded in Stockholm, Sweden. The country's news is reported in English by, among others, *[The Local](/wiki/The_Local "The Local")* (liberal).[[302]](#cite_note-312)

The public broadcasting companies held a monopoly on radio and television for a long time in Sweden. Licence-funded radio broadcasts started in 1925. A second radio network was started in 1954, and a third opened 1962, in response to [pirate radio](/wiki/Pirate_radio "Pirate radio") stations. Non-profit [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio#Sweden "Community radio") was allowed in 1979 and in 1993 commercial local radio started.

The licence-funded television service was officially launched in 1956. A second channel, [TV2](/wiki/SVT2 "SVT2"), was launched in 1969. These two channels (operated by [Sveriges Television](/wiki/Sveriges_Television "Sveriges Television") since the late 1970s) held a monopoly until the 1980s when cable and satellite television became available. The first Swedish-language satellite service was [TV3](/wiki/TV3_(Sweden) "TV3 (Sweden)") which started broadcasting from London in 1987. It was followed by [Kanal 5](/wiki/Kanal_5_(Sweden) "Kanal 5 (Sweden)") in 1989 (then known as Nordic Channel) and [TV4](/wiki/TV4_(Sweden) "TV4 (Sweden)") in 1990. TV4 began its terrestrial broadcasts in 1992, becoming the first private channel to broadcast television content from within the country.

Around half the population are connected to cable television. [Digital terrestrial television in Sweden](/wiki/Digital_terrestrial_television_in_Sweden "Digital terrestrial television in Sweden") started in 1999.

### Literature

Main article: [Swedish literature](/wiki/Swedish_literature "Swedish literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Portrait_of_August_Strindberg_by_Richard_Bergh_1905.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_August_Strindberg_by_Richard_Bergh_1905.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_August_Strindberg_by_Richard_Bergh_1905.jpg)

The writer and playwright [August Strindberg](/wiki/August_Strindberg "August Strindberg")

Sweden has many authors of worldwide recognition including [August Strindberg](/wiki/August_Strindberg "August Strindberg"), [Astrid Lindgren](/wiki/Astrid_Lindgren "Astrid Lindgren"), and Nobel Prize winners [Selma Lagerlöf](/wiki/Selma_Lagerl%C3%B6f "Selma Lagerlöf") and [Harry Martinson](/wiki/Harry_Martinson "Harry Martinson"). In total seven [Nobel Prizes in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") have been awarded to Swedes.
The first literary text from Sweden is the [Rök runestone](/wiki/R%C3%B6k_runestone "Rök runestone"), carved during the Viking Age c. 800 AD. With the conversion of the land to Christianity around 1100 AD, Sweden entered the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), during which monastic writers preferred to use Latin. Therefore, there are only a few texts in [Old Swedish](/wiki/Old_Swedish "Old Swedish") from that period. Swedish literature only began to flourish when the language was standardised during the 16th century. This standardisation was largely due to the full translation of the Bible into Swedish in 1541. This translation is the so-called [Gustav Vasa Bible](/wiki/Gustav_Vasa_Bible "Gustav Vasa Bible").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Inger_Nilsson_as_Pippi_L%C3%A5ngstrump.jpg/250px-Inger_Nilsson_as_Pippi_L%C3%A5ngstrump.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inger_Nilsson_as_Pippi_L%C3%A5ngstrump.jpg)

[Pippi Longstocking](/wiki/Pippi_Longstocking "Pippi Longstocking"), main character in a [series of children's books](/wiki/Pippi_Longstocking_(book) "Pippi Longstocking (book)") by writer [Astrid Lindgren](/wiki/Astrid_Lindgren "Astrid Lindgren")

With improved education and the freedom brought by [secularisation](/wiki/Secularisation "Secularisation"), the 17th century saw several notable authors develop the Swedish language further. Some key figures include [Georg Stiernhielm](/wiki/Georg_Stiernhielm "Georg Stiernhielm") (17th century), who was the first to write classical poetry in Swedish; [Johan Henric Kellgren](/wiki/Johan_Henric_Kellgren "Johan Henric Kellgren") (18th century), the first to write fluent Swedish prose; Carl Michael Bellman (late 18th century), the first writer of [burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque "Burlesque") ballads; and August Strindberg (late 19th century), a socio-realistic writer and playwright who won worldwide fame. The early 20th century continued to produce notable authors, such as Selma Lagerlöf, (Nobel laureate 1909), [Verner von Heidenstam](/wiki/Verner_von_Heidenstam "Verner von Heidenstam") (Nobel laureate 1916) and [Pär Lagerkvist](/wiki/P%C3%A4r_Lagerkvist "Pär Lagerkvist") (Nobel laureate 1951).

In recent decades, a handful of Swedish writers have established themselves internationally, including the detective novelist [Henning Mankell](/wiki/Henning_Mankell "Henning Mankell") and the writer of spy fiction [Jan Guillou](/wiki/Jan_Guillou "Jan Guillou"). The Swedish writer to have made the most lasting impression on world literature is the children's book writer Astrid Lindgren, and her books about [Pippi Longstocking](/wiki/Pippi_Longstocking "Pippi Longstocking"), [Emil](/wiki/Emil_i_L%C3%B6nneberga "Emil i Lönneberga"), and others. In 2008, the second best-selling fiction author in the world was [Stieg Larsson](/wiki/Stieg_Larsson "Stieg Larsson"), whose *Millennium* series of crime novels is being published posthumously to critical acclaim.[[303]](#cite_note-313)

### Holidays

Main article: [Public holidays in Sweden](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Sweden "Public holidays in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Valborg_2132_%287135992133%29.jpg/250px-Valborg_2132_%287135992133%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valborg_2132_(7135992133).jpg)

[Walpurgis Night](/wiki/Walpurgis_Night "Walpurgis Night") bonfire in Sweden

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Lucia_2018_NZ7_1187_%2846327820671%29.jpg/250px-Lucia_2018_NZ7_1187_%2846327820671%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lucia_2018_NZ7_1187_(46327820671).jpg)

St. Lucy's Day celebration in [Vaxholm](/wiki/Vaxholm "Vaxholm"), 2018

Apart from traditional Protestant [Christian holidays](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year"), Sweden also celebrates some unique holidays, some of a pre-Christian tradition. They include [Midsummer](/wiki/Midsummer "Midsummer") celebrating the summer [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice"); [Walpurgis Night](/wiki/Walpurgis_Night "Walpurgis Night") (*Valborgsmässoafton*) on 30 April lighting bonfires; and Labour Day or May Day on 1 May is dedicated to socialist demonstrations. The day of giver-of-light [Saint Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucy "Saint Lucy"), 13 December, is widely acknowledged in elaborate celebrations which betoken its Italian origin and commence the month-long Christmas season.

6 June is the [National Day of Sweden](/wiki/National_holiday_of_Sweden "National holiday of Sweden") and has since 2005 been a public holiday. Furthermore, there are [official flag flying day](/wiki/Flag_flying_days_in_Sweden "Flag flying days in Sweden") observances and a [Namesdays in Sweden](/wiki/Namesdays_in_Sweden "Namesdays in Sweden") calendar. In August many Swedes have *kräftskivor* (crayfish dinner parties). [Martin of Tours](/wiki/Martin_of_Tours "Martin of Tours") Eve is celebrated in [Scania](/wiki/Scania "Scania") in November with *Mårten Gås* parties, where roast goose and *[svartsoppa](/wiki/Svartsoppa "Svartsoppa")* ('black soup') are served. The [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people"), one of Sweden's indigenous minorities, have their holiday on 6 February and Scania celebrate their Scanian Flag day on the third Sunday in July.[[304]](#cite_note-314)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Sweden](/wiki/Cinema_of_Sweden "Cinema of Sweden")

Swedish 20th-century culture is noted by pioneering works in the early days of cinema, with [Mauritz Stiller](/wiki/Mauritz_Stiller "Mauritz Stiller") and [Victor Sjöström](/wiki/Victor_Sj%C3%B6str%C3%B6m "Victor Sjöström"). In the 1920s–1980s, the filmmaker [Ingmar Bergman](/wiki/Ingmar_Bergman "Ingmar Bergman") and actors [Greta Garbo](/wiki/Greta_Garbo "Greta Garbo") and [Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman") became internationally noted people within cinema. More recently, the films of [Lukas Moodysson](/wiki/Lukas_Moodysson "Lukas Moodysson"), [Lasse Hallström](/wiki/Lasse_Hallstr%C3%B6m "Lasse Hallström"), and [Ruben Östlund](/wiki/Ruben_%C3%96stlund "Ruben Östlund") have received international recognition.

### Cuisine

Main article: [Swedish cuisine](/wiki/Swedish_cuisine "Swedish cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Sveriges_Nationaldag_2014.jpg/250px-Sveriges_Nationaldag_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sveriges_Nationaldag_2014.jpg)

[Cinnamon rolls](/wiki/Cinnamon_roll "Cinnamon roll") originated in Sweden and Denmark.

Swedish cuisine, like that of the other [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") ([Denmark](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Denmark "Cuisine of Denmark"), [Norway](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Norway "Cuisine of Norway") and [Finland](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Finland "Cuisine of Finland")), was traditionally simple. Fish (particularly [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring")), meat, potatoes and [dairy products](/wiki/Dairy_products "Dairy products") played prominent roles. Spices were sparse. Preparations include Swedish meatballs, traditionally served with gravy, boiled potatoes and [lingonberry jam](/wiki/Lingonberry_jam "Lingonberry jam"); [pancakes](/wiki/Swedish_pancakes "Swedish pancakes"); *[pyttipanna](/wiki/Pyttipanna "Pyttipanna")*, a spiced fried hash of meat and potatoes originally meant to use up any leftovers of meat; *[lutfisk](/wiki/Lutefisk "Lutefisk")*; and the *[smörgåsbord](/wiki/Sm%C3%B6rg%C3%A5sbord "Smörgåsbord")*, or lavish buffet. *[Akvavit](/wiki/Akvavit "Akvavit")* is a popular alcoholic [distilled beverage](/wiki/Distilled_beverage "Distilled beverage"), and the drinking of *[snaps](/wiki/Snaps "Snaps")* is of cultural importance. The traditional flat and dry [crisp bread](/wiki/Crisp_bread "Crisp bread") has developed into several contemporary variants. Regionally important foods are the *[surströmming](/wiki/Surstr%C3%B6mming "Surströmming")* (a fermented fish) in northern Sweden and [eel](/wiki/Eel "Eel") in southern Sweden.

In August, at the traditional feast known as crayfish party, *[kräftskiva](/wiki/Kr%C3%A4ftskiva "Kräftskiva")*, Swedes eat large amounts of [crayfish](/wiki/Crayfish "Crayfish") boiled with dill.

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Sweden](/wiki/Sport_in_Sweden "Sport in Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Bj%C3%B6rn_Borg_in_aktie%2C_Bestanddeelnr_930-2090.jpg/250px-Bj%C3%B6rn_Borg_in_aktie%2C_Bestanddeelnr_930-2090.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bj%C3%B6rn_Borg_in_aktie,_Bestanddeelnr_930-2090.jpg)

Former world No. 1 tennis player [Björn Borg](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rn_Borg "Björn Borg")

Sport activities are a national movement with half of the population actively participating in organised sporting activities. The two main spectator sports are [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"). Second to football, [horse sports](/wiki/Horse_sports "Horse sports") (of which most of the participants are women) have the highest number of practitioners. Thereafter, [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [orienteering](/wiki/Orienteering "Orienteering"), [gymnastics](/wiki/Gymnastics "Gymnastics"), [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field"), and the [team sports](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") of ice hockey, [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), [floorball](/wiki/Floorball "Floorball"), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") and [bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy") are the most popular in terms of practitioners.[[305]](#cite_note-315)
The [Swedish national men's ice hockey team](/wiki/Swedish_national_men%27s_ice_hockey_team "Swedish national men's ice hockey team"), affectionately known as *Tre Kronor* (English: [Three Crowns](/wiki/Three_Crowns "Three Crowns")), has won the [World Championships](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships") nine times, placing them third in the all-time medal count. *Tre Kronor* also won Olympic gold medals in [1994](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [2006](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2006 Winter Olympics"). In 2006, *Tre Kronor* became the first national hockey team to win both the Olympic and world championships in the same year. The [Swedish national men's football team](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") has seen some success at the World Cup in the past, finishing second when they hosted the tournament in 1958, and third twice, in [1950](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup") and [1994](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup").

Sweden hosted the [1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics"), [Equestrian at the 1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian at the 1956 Summer Olympics") and the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") in [1958](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup "1958 FIFA World Cup"). Other big sports events include the [UEFA Euro 1992](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1992 "UEFA Euro 1992"), [1995 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/1995_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "1995 FIFA Women's World Cup"), [1995 World Championships in Athletics](/wiki/1995_World_Championships_in_Athletics "1995 World Championships in Athletics"), [UEFA Women's Euro 2013](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Euro_2013 "UEFA Women's Euro 2013"), and several championships of ice hockey, [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling"), athletics, [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing"), [bandy](/wiki/Bandy_World_Championship "Bandy World Championship"), [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating") and swimming.
